summary:
Pinnacle Point is a series of cave sites on the Cape coast of South Africa near Mossel Bay, Western Cape, most famous for archaeological evidence of early modern human behavior dated to approximately 164,000–90,000 BCE — some of the oldest known evidence for symbolic behavior, ochre use, and shellfish exploitation by modern humans. By 10,000–8,000 BCE, the Pinnacle Point caves were occupied by communities of the Later Stone Age, the terminal phase of the African Stone Age cultural sequence.

The Later Stone Age occupation at Pinnacle Point during the period of this era falls within the broader context of the [[event:1374|Wilton Industry]] of southern Africa, characterized by a microlithic technology and broad-spectrum hunter-gatherer-fisher subsistence. The cave's coastal location provided access to both marine shellfish beds and the rich biodiversity of the Cape fynbos biome. At 10,000 BCE, the coastline was still some distance from the current shore due to lower sea levels, and as sea levels rose with the [[event:1262|end of the Ice Age]], the productive coastal resource zones gradually approached the caves, potentially enhancing their attractiveness as occupation sites.

images.caption: Excavations in progress in one of the Pinnacle Point Caves, 2011
